name: Washington DC
examName: District of Columbia Real Estate Salesperson Examination

examFacts.notes: Administered by PSI for the District of Columbia Real Estate Commission (DCREC), housed within the Department of Licensing and Consumer Protection (DLCP).

intro:
The Washington DC real estate salesperson exam is one of the toughest in the country. The DC Real Estate Commission (DCREC), housed within the Department of Licensing and Consumer Protection, contracts with PSI to administer it as a 110-question test split 80 national and 30 DC-specific, with 210 minutes of seat time. You need a scaled 75 on each portion separately, so a strong national score will not rescue a weak DC portion, and DC-specific prep is the only way to clear that 30-question state half on the first try.

State-specific prep matters more here than almost anywhere else. The DC portion leans heavily on the DCREC's own statutes and rules, including the Real Estate Guaranty and Education Fund, the DCREC advertising requirements at 17 DCMR § 2615, the Tenant Opportunity to Purchase Act, rent control under the Rental Housing Act, and the broader fair-housing protected classes under the DC Human Rights Act of 1977. None of that looks like the material a candidate in a neighboring state studies. A generic real estate practice test won't cut it. You need Washington DC-specific practice questions.

Below are 20 free Washington DC real estate test questions and answers from RealReady's full Washington DC bank. Each tests a specific topic with the correct answer and a plain-English explanation under the card. Read each one, commit to an answer, then reveal. Treat it like a real DCREC practice test, and the ones you miss show you what to focus on next.

Get the RealReady app on iPhone, iPad, or Android for the rest of the Washington DC question bank. The full app includes short articles that walk you through the why behind each topic, detailed explanations on every question, a missed-question drill mode, and progress tracking that shows your per-category accuracy.

faq.q: How many questions are on the DC real estate exam?
faq.a: The District of Columbia Real Estate Salesperson Examination has 110 scored questions, split into an 80-item national portion and a 30-item DC-specific portion. PSI administers both portions in one sitting at a DC test site, and the two portions are scored separately.

faq.q: What's the passing score for the DC real estate exam?
faq.a: 75% on each portion separately. PSI uses scaled scoring rather than a literal raw percentage, but plan to clear roughly 60 of 80 on the national side and 23 of 30 on the DC side to be safely above the line. If you pass one portion and fail the other, you only need to retake the failed portion, and you have six months from your first attempt to clear it.

faq.q: How much does the DC real estate exam cost?
faq.a: PSI charges $61.50 per examination attempt, and that single fee covers both portions whether you sit them together or retake one. After passing, the DC salesperson license fee is $130 for an initial two-year term per DCREC, with renewal also $130 on the same two-year cycle.

faq.q: How long is the DC real estate exam?
faq.a: 210 minutes of scored time total, broken into 120 minutes for the national portion and 90 minutes for the DC portion. PSI delivers both portions back-to-back at the DC testing center, and you find out whether you passed before you leave.

faq.q: Is the DC real estate exam hard?
faq.a: The split-portion format is where most candidates trip up. They over-prepare on national real estate principles and underestimate the 30-question DC portion, which is densely DC-specific (DCREC enforcement powers, the Real Estate Guaranty and Education Fund, advertising rules under 17 DCMR, the DC Human Rights Act's expanded fair-housing protections, rent control, and the Tenant Opportunity to Purchase Act). The questions are written to test whether you actually understand DC real estate law, not to trick you. Honest practice on DC-specific questions is the difference between a first-time pass and a $61.50 retake.

faq.q: What's on the DC real estate exam?
faq.a: The 80-question national portion covers ten content areas, with contracts (about 17%), practice of real estate (about 13%), general principles of agency (about 13%), financing (about 10%), and real estate calculations (about 10%) carrying the heaviest weights. The 30-question DC portion is heavier still on state law. Half of it tests statutory requirements governing licensee activity (advertising, handling of monies, agency, commissions, fair housing, tenant's right to purchase, property disclosures), and the remaining 50% splits between DCREC's duties and powers (about 25%) and DC licensing rules (about 25%).

faq.q: What's the best way to prepare for the DC real estate exam?
faq.a: After finishing your DCREC-approved pre-license course, the highest-leverage thing you can do is grind through DC-specific practice questions with honest feedback on every miss. Generic real estate practice tests will help with national principles but will not catch you on DC's unique rules, especially the half of the state portion that hangs on statutory requirements governing licensee activity. The RealReady app gives you the full bank of DC questions, plus progress tracking and a missed-question mode so you can drill what you're weak on. Most users study 30 to 60 minutes a day for 2 to 4 weeks before sitting the DC exam.
